content.problemStatement:
You are given a **binary** string SSS of length NNN.

You have to construct a binary string XXX, of length NNN, such that:


For each (1≤i<N)(1\le i \lt N)(1≤i<N), X(i+1)=(Xi⊕Si)X_{(i+1)} = (X_i \oplus S_i)X(i+1)​=(Xi​⊕Si​), where ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation;
The count of 111s in the string XXX is **maximised**.
Find the **maximum** count of 111 in string XXX.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the length of the string SSS.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the **maximum** count of 111 in string XXX.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the binary string S. The second line contains the binary string S of length N consisting only of characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the maximum possible number of occurrences of the substring "111" in a binary string X that satisfies X_{i+1}=X_i XOR S_i for all 1 ≤ i < N.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we try both possible first bits of X. The best choice yields the shown number of "111" substrings.
Starting with X1=1 gives X = 111, which contains exactly one occurrence of "111". Starting with 0 gives X = 000, containing none.
Both possible X strings (01100 and 10011) contain no "111" substring, so the maximum is 0.

solution.approach: The relation X_{i+1}=X_i⊕S_i uniquely determines the whole string X once the first character X_1 is chosen. Therefore only two candidates exist: one with X_1=0 and one with X_1=1. For each candidate we can construct X in O(N) time and count how many times the pattern "111" appears (i.e., for every i from 1 to N‑2 check if X[i],X[i+1],X[i+2] are all '1'). The answer for the test case is the larger of the two counts. Edge case: if N<3 the count is always 0.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the temporary X array (can be reduced to O(1) by keeping only the last two bits, but O(N) fits the limits).

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        S = next(it).strip()
        best = 0
        for start in (0, 1):
            prev = start
            w1 = w2 = -1
            cnt = 0
            for i in range(N):
                cur = prev if i == 0 else prev ^ (ord(S[i-1]) - 48)
                if i >= 2 and w1 == 1 and w2 == 1 and cur == 1:
                    cnt += 1
                w1, w2 = w2, cur
                prev = cur
            if cnt > best:
                best = cnt
        out_lines.append(str(best))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
